blocks.md:
## 1. Requirements (Functional + Non-Functional)

> 🎙️ "Here's how I'll structure this: I'll lock the requirements and rough scale, sketch the API and data model, draw the high-level design one requirement at a time, then go deep on the two or three hardest parts — fan-out, delivery guarantees, and rate-limiting/preferences — checking in with you as I go. Sound good?"

The core tension: a notification system is a **fan-out + delivery engine**. One event ("your order shipped") can explode into millions of messages across email, push, and SMS, each with a different provider, latency profile, and failure mode — and we must deliver them fast, exactly the way each user asked for, without ever spamming them. I'll target an **Amazon-style bar** (cost and operations are first-class, work backwards from the customer) — tell me if you'd rather I tilt toward Meta-style raw scale.

**Functional (the core flows — "above the line"):**

**F1. A producer service can trigger a notification for a user/event.**
- 🗣️ Plain words: some other system (orders, payments, social) says "send this person a message about X," and we take it from there — the producer doesn't care how it reaches the user.
- ⚡ Why it's hard: one logical trigger can fan out to many users and many channels; we have to accept the trigger in milliseconds and do the heavy lifting asynchronously, or the calling service slows down.

**F2. The system delivers across multiple channels — push, email, SMS.**
- 🗣️ Plain words: the same alert might go as a phone push, an email, and a text, and each one goes out through a different outside company (APNs/FCM for push, SES/SendGrid for email, Twilio for SMS).
- ⚡ Why it's hard: every channel has its own format, auth, throughput limit, and failure behavior — and the providers themselves go down or throttle us, so we need per-channel adapters and retries.

**F3. Users can customize preferences — which types they get, on which channels, how often.**
- 🗣️ Plain words: a user can say "only send me security alerts by SMS, marketing only by email, and never more than one digest a day."
- ⚡ Why it's hard: preferences must be checked on the hot path for every single message at huge volume, and "frequency" means we need throttling/digest state per user, not just a yes/no flag.

**F4. Real-time delivery for high-priority notifications.**
- 🗣️ Plain words: a 2-factor login code or a fraud alert needs to hit the phone in a second or two, while a weekly newsletter can wait minutes.
- ⚡ Why it's hard: we can't put a 2FA code behind the same queue as a million marketing emails — we need priority lanes so urgent traffic never waits behind bulk traffic.

**F5. Analytics on engagement and effectiveness (sent, delivered, opened, clicked).**
- 🗣️ Plain words: product teams want to see how many messages went out, how many actually landed, and how many people tapped them.
- ⚡ Why it's hard: this is the highest-volume table in the system (an event per message per state change) and must be ingested without slowing the delivery path.

**Below the line (out of scope for today):** in-app notification inbox UI, rich templating/localization editor, A/B testing of message copy, billing/cost-attribution per team, GDPR data-export tooling (I'll mention retention but not build the export pipeline).

**Non-Functional:**

**N1. Scalable to spikes — handle flash events/promotions.**
- 🗣️ Plain words: on a normal day we send a steady stream, but Black Friday or a breaking-news blast can be 50x that for an hour, and nothing should fall over.
- 💥 What breaks without it: a promo blast backs up the queue, 2FA codes get stuck behind marketing mail, and users can't log in — the urgent traffic dies because of the bulk traffic.

**N2. High availability — 99.95% on the ingestion/accept path.**
- 🗣️ Plain words: 99.95% means under ~4.4 hours of downtime per year on accepting triggers; we never want to *lose* a trigger even if delivery is delayed.
- 💥 What breaks without it: if the accept endpoint is down, the order service's "send receipt" call fails and either blocks checkout or silently drops the receipt — a customer pays and hears nothing.

**N3. Durability — an accepted notification is never silently lost (RPO ≈ 0 for accepted triggers).**
- 🗣️ Plain words: once we say "got it" to a producer, that message will eventually go out even if a server crashes one second later.
- 💥 What breaks without it: a crash loses in-flight 2FA codes and password resets — users get locked out and trust collapses.

**N4. Low latency on the priority lane — p99 under ~2s from accept to provider hand-off for high-priority.**
- 🗣️ Plain words: 99 out of 100 urgent messages leave our system to the provider within two seconds.
- 💥 What breaks without it: 2FA codes arrive after the login screen times out; the feature is effectively broken.

**N5. No duplicate spam / honor frequency caps (at-least-once delivery + dedup → effectively-once user experience).**
- 🗣️ Plain words: even though we retry on failure, the user should not get the same alert five times.
- 💥 What breaks without it: a retry storm sends a user 20 copies of one push at 3am; they disable notifications entirely and we lose the channel forever.

⚠️ Gaps I'd flag out loud: true *exactly-once* delivery is impossible across third-party providers (they can deliver and fail to ack); I'll get to **effectively-once via idempotency keys + dedup**, and I'll name the residual duplicate window in the ledger.

> 💬 "The whole game here is fan-out and delivery guarantees — accept fast and durably, then deliver across flaky third parties without spamming anyone."

> 🎙️ Script: "So at its heart this is a fan-out and delivery engine. Producers fire an event, we accept it instantly and durably, then we explode it into per-user, per-channel messages, check each user's preferences, and hand off to the right provider — push, email, or SMS. The two things I care about most are not losing anything we've accepted, and never spamming a user even though we retry. I'll keep urgent traffic like 2FA on its own fast lane so a marketing blast can never delay a login code."

## 2. Clarifying Questions & Assumptions

**1. What scale are we at — roughly how many notifications per day, and what's the peak-to-average ratio?**
- 🗣️ Why I'm asking: the design barely changes at 1M/day but completely changes at 1B/day with 50x spikes — the spike ratio decides whether I need elastic workers and priority queues at all.
- ↔️ Design fork: If high spikes → hard commit to a durable queue (Kafka) + autoscaling workers + priority lanes. If steady low volume → a single managed queue (SQS) and a couple of workers is plenty.
- Assumption I'll proceed with: ~1B notifications/day, ~50x burst on promo events. That's the interesting design.

**2. Is delivery best-effort or must accepted triggers be guaranteed (durability requirement)?**
- 🗣️ Why I'm asking: if a dropped marketing email is fine but a dropped 2FA code is not, I need per-priority durability, which changes how I ack producers.
- ↔️ Design fork: If guaranteed → persist to a durable log before acking the producer (RPO≈0). If best-effort → I can ack from memory and accept some loss for lower cost.
- Assumption I'll proceed with: accepted triggers are guaranteed durable; delivery is at-least-once.

**3. Who owns user contact info and device tokens — us or an upstream identity service?**
- 🗣️ Why I'm asking: if we own device tokens we need token lifecycle management (registration, expiry, cleanup of dead tokens); if upstream owns it we just call out.
- ↔️ Design fork: If we own it → a Device/Token store with a registration API and dead-token reaping. If upstream → a synchronous lookup with a cache.
- Assumption I'll proceed with: we own device tokens and contact channels (it's the richer design).

**4. How strict are frequency caps — best-effort throttling or a hard contractual cap?**
- 🗣️ Why I'm asking: a hard cap ("max 1 marketing msg/day, ever") needs strongly-consistent per-user counters; a soft cap can be eventual and cheaper.
- ↔️ Design fork: If hard → per-user rate state in a strongly-consistent store (Redis with atomic counters + persistence). If soft → approximate counting is fine.
- Assumption I'll proceed with: hard caps for marketing, no cap for transactional/security.

**5. Do we need read-your-own-writes on preferences — i.e. if a user toggles "no SMS," must the very next message respect it instantly?**
- 🗣️ Why I'm asking: if preference changes must be instant, I can't rely on an eventually-consistent cache for the preference check.
- ↔️ Design fork: If instant → write-through cache + read-from-primary on the hot path or short TTL. If eventual → cache with a minute TTL is fine.
- Assumption I'll proceed with: preference changes take effect within seconds (short TTL + write-through).

**6. What latency do different priorities need — is there a hard SLA on 2FA?**
- 🗣️ Why I'm asking: it decides whether one queue suffices or I need separate priority lanes with separate worker pools.
- ↔️ Design fork: If hard 2FA SLA → dedicated high-priority topic + reserved workers. If not → single weighted queue.
- Assumption I'll proceed with: high-priority p99 ≤ 2s to provider; bulk can take minutes.

**7. Are notifications fan-out-to-many (one event → millions, like a broadcast) or mostly 1:1 (one event → one user)?**
- 🗣️ Why I'm asking: broadcast fan-out is the single hardest scaling problem here and needs a dedicated fan-out worker tier.
- ↔️ Design fork: If broadcast-heavy → a fan-out service that expands audiences into per-user jobs in batches. If 1:1 → skip the expansion tier.
- Assumption I'll proceed with: both — transactional 1:1 plus marketing broadcasts to large segments.

**8. What analytics latency is acceptable — real-time dashboards or daily rollups?**
- 🗣️ Why I'm asking: real-time engagement needs a streaming pipeline; daily is a cheap batch job.
- ↔️ Design fork: If real-time → stream events to an OLAP store (ClickHouse). If daily → dump to S3 + batch.
- Assumption I'll proceed with: near-real-time (minutes) engagement metrics.

> 🤝 Checkpoint: "I'm going to assume ~1B/day with big promo spikes, guaranteed durability on accept, hard frequency caps for marketing, and a strict 2FA latency lane. Anything there you'd change before I size it?"

## 3. Scale & Capacity (talkable numbers)

Glossary as I go: **QPS** — queries per second, how many requests hit a tier each second. **DAU** — daily active users. **TTL** — time-to-live, how long a cached value lives before it expires. **Fan-out** — turning one event into many per-user messages.

| Metric | Derivation (step-by-step) | Rounded | Talkable phrase | Decision it drives |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Avg send rate | 1B notifications/day ÷ 86,400 s/day ≈ 11,574/s | ~12K/s | "about twelve thousand a second on average" | Baseline worker throughput |
| Peak send rate | ~12K/s × 50 (promo burst) = 578K/s | ~600K/s | "around six hundred thousand a second at peak" | **Forces a durable buffer + autoscaling — a single DB can't absorb this** |
| Accept (ingest) QPS | producers fire triggers; many triggers fan out, so ingest < send. Assume 200M triggers/day ÷ 86,400 ≈ 2,315/s, ×50 peak ≈ 116K/s | ~120K/s peak | "north of a hundred thousand triggers a second at peak" | API gateway + stateless accept tier sizing |
| High-priority share | assume ~5% urgent: 600K/s × 0.05 = 30K/s | ~30K/s | "about thirty thousand urgent a second" | Dedicated priority lane must sustain 30K/s alone |
| Preference checks/s | one check per message = peak send rate | ~600K/s | "six hundred thousand preference reads a second" | **A Redis node ≈ 150K ops/s → must shard the preference cache (≥4–5 nodes)** |
| Analytics events/s | ~4 state events per message (queued, sent, delivered, opened) × 12K/s avg ≈ 48K/s, peak ~2.4M/s | ~50K/s avg | "tens of thousands of analytics events a second" | OLAP store + stream, never the primary DB |
| Storage — delivery log | 1B/day × ~300 bytes × 30-day retention = 9 TB raw; ×3 replication ≈ 27 TB | ~30 TB | "tens of terabytes for a month of logs" | Cheap on object/columnar store; not a bottleneck |

The **one number that forces the design** is **~600K sends/s at peak vs ~12K/s average** — a 50x spike. No synchronous path survives that, so the buffer (a durable queue) is non-negotiable, and the flip threshold is roughly: *if peak ever stayed under ~50K/s I could skip autoscaling and run a fixed worker pool*; above that, elastic workers behind a durable log are mandatory. Storage is a non-issue — tens of TB on columnar/object storage costs little.

> 🎙️ Script: "The headline number is the spike. We average about twelve thousand sends a second, but a promo can push us to six hundred thousand a second for an hour — fifty times normal. That single fact means I can't deliver synchronously; I have to buffer everything in a durable log and let workers drain it as fast as they can scale. And since a Redis node tops out around a hundred fifty thousand ops a second, my preference checks at six hundred thousand a second alone force me to shard the cache. So I'll spend my design budget on the buffer, the worker fan-out, and the priority lanes — not on storage, which is trivially cheap here."

## 4. Core Entities

This is a first draft of the entities — I'll add full field definitions in §7.

**Notification (the trigger / logical message)** — one line: represents a single accepted request to notify someone about an event; owns the content, template id, priority, and dedup/idempotency key.
- 🗣️ Simple analogy: think of it like a work order dropped in an inbox — "tell user 123 their order shipped" — that we'll process and expand.
- ⚠️ Interviewer probe: "How do you stop the same trigger being processed twice on a retry?" → each Notification carries an idempotency key; we dedup on it before fan-out.

**Message (the per-channel delivery attempt)** — one line: the concrete thing we hand to one provider on one channel for one user (one Notification → many Messages); owns channel, recipient address/token, status, and attempt count.
- 🗣️ Simple analogy: if the Notification is "mail this announcement," each Message is one stamped envelope addressed to one person via one route.
- ⚠️ Interviewer probe: "How do you track delivered vs failed?" → each Message has a status state machine (QUEUED→SENT→DELIVERED/FAILED) updated by provider callbacks.

**User & Device/Channel** — one line: the recipient and their reachable endpoints — email addresses, phone numbers, and push tokens per device; owns token validity and platform (iOS/Android).
- 🗣️ Simple analogy: a contact card with several phone numbers and emails, some of which may be disconnected.
- ⚠️ Interviewer probe: "What about dead push tokens?" → providers return "unregistered"; we mark the token invalid and stop sending to it.

**Preference** — one line: per-user rules for which notification types go to which channels and at what frequency; owns opt-in/opt-out and frequency caps.
- 🗣️ Simple analogy: a "do not disturb" sheet — call me only for emergencies, text me never, email me weekly.
- ⚠️ Interviewer probe: "Where do you check this — before or after fan-out?" → after expansion, per Message, against a cached Preference, before queuing to a provider.

**Template** — one line: the reusable, versioned content shell (subject/body with placeholders) a Notification references.
- 🗣️ Simple analogy: a fill-in-the-blanks form letter.
- ⚠️ Interviewer probe: "Why not inline content?" → templates let us localize and update copy without redeploying producers.

**DeliveryEvent (analytics)** — one line: an append-only record of every state change (sent/delivered/opened/clicked) for analytics; owns the engagement timeline.
- 🗣️ Simple analogy: a delivery-tracking log like a parcel's scan history.
- ⚠️ Interviewer probe: "Isn't this huge?" → yes, it's the biggest table; it lives in a columnar OLAP store, never the primary DB.

> 🎙️ Script: "The key entities are the Notification — the logical 'notify this person' request — which fans out into many Messages, one per channel per user. Around those sit the User with their Devices and contact channels, their Preferences, and a versioned Template for the content. The one to watch is the Message, because that's where the state machine and the duplicate-suppression live. And separately I keep a DeliveryEvent stream for analytics, which I'll deliberately keep off the primary database since it's the highest-volume data we have. I'll flesh out all the fields in the data model section."

## 5. API / Interface

Default REST over HTTPS; caller identity comes from a service JWT (a signed token proving which service is calling), never trusted from the body.

| Method + Path | Auth | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| POST /v1/notifications | Service JWT | Trigger a notification |
| GET /v1/users/{id}/preferences | User/Service JWT | Read preferences |
| PUT /v1/users/{id}/preferences | User JWT | Replace preferences |
| POST /v1/devices | User JWT | Register a device token |
| POST /v1/providers/callback/{channel} | Provider HMAC | Delivery status webhook |
| GET /v1/analytics/notifications | Service JWT | Engagement metrics |

**POST /v1/notifications** — POST because it *creates* new delivery state. Key request fields:
- `eventType: string` — e.g. "ORDER_SHIPPED"; drives which template and default priority apply.
- `recipientUserId: string` (or `audienceId` for broadcasts) — who to notify; for broadcasts we expand server-side.
- `templateData: object` — the placeholder values (order number, name); content itself comes from the server-side Template, not the client.
- `idempotencyKey: string` — caller-supplied unique key so a retry doesn't double-send.
- Absent on purpose: the recipient's *address/token* and the rendered body — the server resolves those, so a buggy producer can't email the wrong person or inject content.

```
// Request
POST /v1/notifications
{ "eventType": "ORDER_SHIPPED", "recipientUserId": "u_123",
  "templateData": { "orderId": "o_789" }, "idempotencyKey": "ord-789-shipped" }

// Response 202
{ "notificationId": "ntf_abc", "status": "ACCEPTED" }
```
One key design decision: we return **202 Accepted**, not 200 with a delivery result. Delivery is async — we durably persist + enqueue, then ack. `status` is server-set; the client can't forge "DELIVERED."

**PUT /v1/users/{id}/preferences** — PUT because it *fully replaces* the preference document.
- `channels: object` — per eventType → allowed channels + frequency cap; this is the whole rule set.
- The `{id}` must match the JWT subject — we ignore any user id in the body so a user can't edit someone else's preferences.
```
// Request
PUT /v1/users/u_123/preferences
{ "MARKETING": { "channels": ["email"], "maxPerDay": 1 },
  "SECURITY":  { "channels": ["sms","push"] } }
// Response 200
{ "version": 7, "updatedAt": "2026-06-20T10:00:00Z" }
```
Design decision: a monotonically increasing `version` so the hot path can detect stale cached preferences.

**POST /v1/providers/callback/{channel}** — providers POST delivery receipts (delivered/bounced/opened). Verified by HMAC (a signature proving the call really came from the provider, not a forger). Updates the Message status and emits a DeliveryEvent.

> 💬 "Notice the producer never sends the email address or the body — it sends an event type and an idempotency key, and we resolve everything server-side. That's both security and decoupling."

> 🎙️ Script: "The main endpoint is POST /notifications, and the important thing is it returns 202 Accepted, not a delivery result — we durably store the trigger, enqueue it, and ack in milliseconds, then do the real work asynchronously. The caller sends an event type, who to notify, some template data, and an idempotency key — but never the actual address or rendered content, so we control content and a buggy producer can't leak data. Preferences are a full PUT keyed to the caller's own identity, with a version number so the delivery path can tell if its cached copy is stale. And providers call us back on a signed webhook to tell us what actually got delivered."

## 6. High-Level Design (built one functional requirement at a time)

### 6.1 "A producer can trigger a notification" (F1) + durable accept (N3)

The producer fires an event; we must accept it in milliseconds and never lose it. Start minimal: an Ingestion Service that validates, dedups on the idempotency key, persists, and enqueues.

**ARCHITECTURE DECISIONS TABLE**

| Component | What it is (plain English) | Why THIS choice | What we DIDN'T pick & why not | Trade-off we accept |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| API Gateway | The front door that authenticates callers and rate-limits before traffic hits our services | Need authn + abuse protection at 120K/s without coding it per service | Not a raw load balancer (no auth/rate-limit); not in-app checks (duplicated everywhere) | One more hop (~1-2ms) |
| Ingestion Service | A stateless service that validates, dedups, persists the trigger, and enqueues it | Must accept fast and return 202; statelessness lets it autoscale with the spike | Not writing straight to the DB from the gateway (no validation/dedup) | Extra tier to operate |
| Durable Log (Kafka) | A append-only log that buffers a firehose of events so nothing is lost if consumers are slow | 50x spikes need a buffer that survives crashes and replays; this is the durability spine | Not SQS-only (limited replay/ordering); not in-memory queue (loses data on crash) | Operational complexity of running Kafka |
| Dedup Store (Redis) | An in-memory key store holding seen idempotency keys with a TTL | Atomic "set-if-absent" stops a retried trigger from double-sending | Not a DB unique index alone (slower at 120K/s); Redis is the fast first gate | Dedup window bounded by TTL |

> 🎙️ Narration: "The front door is an API Gateway — that's just the authenticated, rate-limited entry point so I'm not re-coding auth in every service. Behind it is a stateless Ingestion Service whose only job is to validate the trigger, check it's not a duplicate, write it durably, and return 202. The durability spine is Kafka — think of it as an append-only log that buffers a firehose so nothing's lost if my workers fall behind during a spike; I picked it over a plain queue because I need replay and it survives crashes. For dedup I use Redis with an atomic set-if-absent on the idempotency key, which is faster than leaning on a database index at this rate."

**🗣️ Key terms for this slice:**
> **API Gateway** — the authenticated, rate-limiting front door to our services. Here it absorbs auth and abuse-control at 120K/s so individual services don't have to.
> **Kafka** — a durable, replayable append-only log of events. Here it's the buffer that lets us accept a 50x spike without losing anything and drain it at worker speed.
> **Idempotency key** — a caller-supplied unique id for a request. Here it lets a retried trigger be recognized and ignored so we don't double-send.
> **Redis** — an in-memory key-value store, extremely fast. Here it holds recently-seen idempotency keys for sub-millisecond dedup.

**Accept flow (durable ingest)**
```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Producer service]
    P -->|"1: POST /notifications"| GW[API Gateway]
    GW -->|"2: forward"| ING[Ingestion Service]
    ING -->|"3: set-if-absent key"| DDP[(Redis dedup)]
    ING -->|"4: append trigger"| LOG[[Kafka log]]
    ING -->|"5: 202 Accepted"| P
    LOG -.->|"6: consumed later"| FO[Fan-out workers]
    classDef client fill:#1a3a5c,stroke:#4A90D9,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    classDef svc fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    classDef queue fill:#3d2a0b,stroke:#B7791F,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    classDef db fill:#2a2f3a,stroke:#6B7280,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    class P client
    class GW,ING,FO svc
    class LOG queue
    class DDP db
```

**NUMBERED STEP NARRATION**
1. Producer POSTs the trigger. Why the gateway first: it authenticates the service JWT and rate-limits so a misbehaving producer can't flood us. State change: none yet. What if it fails: gateway rejects with 401/429, producer retries.
2. Gateway forwards to Ingestion. Why: Ingestion owns validation/dedup logic. State: none. Failure: if Ingestion is unhealthy the LB routes to another instance (stateless).
3. Ingestion does atomic set-if-absent on the idempotency key in Redis. Why: this is the dedup gate. State: key recorded with TTL. Failure: if the key already exists we short-circuit and return the prior 202 — no double work.
4. Ingestion appends the trigger to Kafka. Why: this is the durable commit — once written, it's guaranteed processed. State: trigger persisted + replicated. Failure: if Kafka write fails we return 503 (do NOT ack) so the producer retries — we never ack without durability.
5. Ingestion returns 202. State: producer is unblocked. Failure: if the producer never sees the 202 it retries with the same key → dedup absorbs it.
6. Fan-out workers consume asynchronously (next slice).

> 💬 "The key insight: I ack only *after* the trigger is durably in Kafka — that's how I guarantee 'accepted means it'll happen' without making the producer wait for delivery."

> 🎙️ Script: "When a producer fires an event, it hits the gateway for auth and rate-limiting, then the Ingestion Service. Ingestion does one atomic dedup check in Redis, writes the trigger durably to Kafka, and only then returns 202. That ordering is the whole durability story — I never tell the producer 'got it' until the message is safely replicated in the log, so a crash a millisecond later still results in delivery. The trade-off is operating Kafka, which is worth it for a 50x spike buffer with replay."

### 6.2 "Deliver across push, email, SMS" (F2) + preferences (F3)

Now we drain the log: a Fan-out Service expands a trigger into per-user, per-channel Messages, each checked against Preferences, then routed to a channel-specific Worker that talks to the provider.

**ARCHITECTURE DECISIONS TABLE**

| Component | What it is (plain English) | Why THIS choice | What we DIDN'T pick & why not | Trade-off we accept |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Fan-out Service | A worker that turns one trigger into many per-user Messages (esp. broadcasts) | Broadcasts (1 event → millions) need batched expansion off the hot path | Not expanding in Ingestion (would block the accept path) | Adds a processing tier + latency |
| Preference Service + cache | Looks up per-user channel/frequency rules, cached in Redis | Must filter every Message at 600K/s; cache makes it sub-ms | Not hitting Postgres per message (can't do 600K/s reads) | Cache staleness bounded by short TTL |
| Channel Workers | Per-channel adapters (push/email/SMS) that format + call the provider and handle retries | Each provider has its own API, auth, throttle, retry semantics | Not one generic sender (can't honor per-provider limits/retries) | More code paths to maintain |
| Provider adapters | Thin clients for APNs/FCM, SES/SendGrid, Twilio | Isolate third-party quirks + let us swap providers | Not calling providers inline from fan-out (couples us to their latency) | Extra abstraction layer |

> 🎙️ Narration: "Fan-out is its own tier because a single broadcast can become millions of messages, and I don't want that expansion blocking the accept path — so it runs as workers draining Kafka in batches. Before anything goes out, each message hits the Preference Service, which I back with a Redis cache because I'm doing six hundred thousand checks a second and Postgres simply can't serve reads at that rate. Then per-channel workers — push, email, SMS — format the message and call the provider, each with its own retry and throttle logic, because APNs, SES, and Twilio behave completely differently. I keep thin provider adapters so I can swap SendGrid for SES without touching the rest."

**🗣️ Key terms for this slice:**
> **Fan-out** — expanding one logical event into many individual messages. Here it handles broadcasts so a single promo becomes millions of per-user jobs without blocking ingestion.
> **APNs / FCM** — Apple's and Google's push notification gateways. Here they're the providers our push worker hands off to for phone notifications.
> **Provider adapter** — a thin wrapper around a third-party API. Here it isolates each provider's quirks so we can retry, throttle, or swap providers independently.
> **Write-through cache** — a cache updated at the same time as the database. Here it keeps preference reads sub-millisecond while staying fresh.

**Delivery / fan-out flow**
```mermaid
flowchart TD
    LOG[[Kafka log]]
    LOG -->|"1: consume trigger"| FO[Fan-out Service]
    FO -->|"2: per-user Messages"| MQ[[Channel queues by priority]]
    MQ -->|"3: dequeue Message"| CW[Channel Worker]
    CW -->|"4: check prefs"| PREF[(Redis pref cache)]
    PREF -.->|"4b: miss"| PDB[(Postgres prefs)]
    CW -->|"5: send"| PROV[Provider APNs/FCM/SES/Twilio]
    PROV -.->|"6: callback"| CB[Callback Service]
    CB -->|"7: status + event"| ES[[Analytics stream]]
    classDef client fill:#1a3a5c,stroke:#4A90D9,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    classDef svc fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    classDef queue fill:#3d2a0b,stroke:#B7791F,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    classDef db fill:#2a2f3a,stroke:#6B7280,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    classDef ext fill:#3a1a4a,stroke:#6B46C1,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    class FO,CW,CB svc
    class LOG,MQ,ES queue
    class PREF,PDB db
    class PROV ext
```
↳ reuses existing: Kafka log (from §6.1)

**NUMBERED STEP NARRATION**
1. Fan-out consumes a trigger. Why: it owns audience expansion. State: trigger marked in-progress (offset tracked by Kafka). Failure: worker crash → Kafka redelivers from last committed offset (at-least-once).
2. Fan-out writes per-user Messages onto channel queues split by priority. Why: priority split keeps 2FA ahead of bulk. State: N Messages created (QUEUED). Failure: partial expansion → idempotent by (notificationId,userId,channel) so re-run doesn't duplicate.
3. A Channel Worker dequeues a Message. Why: per-channel concurrency control. State: Message → SENDING. Failure: visibility timeout returns it to the queue if the worker dies.
4. Worker checks preferences in Redis (miss → Postgres, then backfill). Why: enforce opt-outs/caps at the last moment so a just-changed pref is honored. State: Message may be SUPPRESSED. Failure: cache down → fall back to Postgres read (slower but correct).
5. Worker calls the provider via the adapter. Why: provider owns actual delivery. State: Message → SENT, attempt++. Failure: provider 5xx/throttle → exponential backoff retry; permanent error (bad token) → mark FAILED + reap token.
6. Provider later calls our Callback Service. Why: only the provider knows true delivery. State: Message → DELIVERED/BOUNCED. Failure: missing callback → after timeout we treat as unknown, optionally re-probe.
7. Callback emits a DeliveryEvent to the analytics stream (§6.3).

> 💬 "I check preferences at the very last step before sending — not at ingest — so a user who just turned off SMS doesn't get one that was already in flight."

> 🎙️ Script: "Workers drain the Kafka log. The Fan-out Service expands a trigger into one Message per user per channel and drops them onto priority-split queues. A channel worker picks each up, checks the user's preferences in a Redis cache at the last possible moment so opt-outs are honored, then calls the right provider through a thin adapter that owns retries and throttling. The provider later calls our webhook to tell us what actually landed, and we record that. The key decision is the last-moment preference check plus per-channel workers — it costs an extra hop but it's the only way to honor instant opt-outs and respect each provider's limits."

### 6.3 "Real-time priority delivery" (F4) + "Analytics" (F5)

↳ builds on §6.2 diagram — the new parts are the **priority lanes** and the **analytics pipeline**, already shown as "Channel queues by priority" and "Analytics stream" above.

Priority: we run **separate Kafka topics / queues per priority** (HIGH, DEFAULT, BULK) with **reserved worker pools** for HIGH, so 30K/s of 2FA never queues behind 570K/s of marketing. Analytics: the Callback + worker emit DeliveryEvents to a stream consumed into **ClickHouse** (a columnar OLAP database — stores data by column so aggregate queries over billions of rows are fast).

**ARCHITECTURE DECISIONS TABLE**

| Component | What it is (plain English) | Why THIS choice | What we DIDN'T pick & why not | Trade-off we accept |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Priority lanes | Separate queues + reserved workers per priority class | Guarantees 2FA latency under a marketing blast | Not one weighted queue (bulk can still starve urgent under load) | Idle reserved capacity for HIGH |
| Analytics stream | A Kafka topic of delivery events | Decouples high-volume event capture from delivery | Not writing events to Postgres (50K-2.4M/s would melt it) | Eventual (minutes) analytics |
| ClickHouse (OLAP) | Columnar DB built for fast aggregations over huge tables | Engagement queries over billions of rows in sub-second | Not Postgres (row store, slow scans); not Elasticsearch (costlier for pure aggregates) | Not for point lookups/transactions |

**🗣️ Key terms for this slice:**
> **Priority lane** — a dedicated queue + workers for one urgency class. Here it keeps 2FA codes flowing during a marketing flood.
> **ClickHouse** — a columnar database optimized for analytics over enormous tables. Here it answers "how many delivered/opened" across billions of events fast.
> **OLAP** — Online Analytical Processing, i.e. aggregation-heavy querying. Here it's the engagement-metrics workload.

**NUMBERED STEP NARRATION (priority path)**
1. Fan-out tags each Message with a priority derived from eventType and routes HIGH to the high topic. Why: classification at expansion time. State: Message on a specific lane. Failure: misclassification → default to DEFAULT lane (safe).
2. Reserved HIGH workers poll only the high topic. Why: guaranteed throughput for urgent traffic. State: 2FA sent within p99 2s. Failure: HIGH workers saturated → autoscale first, alarm on lane lag.
3. DeliveryEvents stream to ClickHouse via a consumer. Why: keep analytics off the hot path. State: dashboards update within minutes. Failure: consumer lag → events buffer in Kafka, no data loss.

> 💬 "Urgent traffic gets its own road, not a faster car in the same traffic jam — separate topics and reserved workers, so a promo can't delay a login code."

> 🎙️ Script: "For real-time priority I don't use one clever weighted queue — I physically separate the lanes into different topics with their own reserved workers, so even at six hundred thousand a second of marketing, the thirty thousand a second of 2FA flows on an empty road and hits its two-second budget. For analytics, every delivery event streams into ClickHouse, a columnar store that can aggregate billions of rows fast, and crucially it's off the delivery path — if the analytics consumer lags, events just buffer in Kafka and nothing is lost."

**Final (high-level)** — reconciled union of all slices.
```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Producer service]
    U[User device]
    P -->|"trigger"| GW[API Gateway]
    U -->|"prefs / register"| GW
    GW --> ING[Ingestion Service]
    ING --> DDP[(Redis dedup)]
    ING -->|"durable append"| LOG[[Kafka triggers]]
    subgraph Write["Fan-out / Delivery path"]
      LOG --> FO[Fan-out Service]
      FO --> MQ[[Priority queues HIGH/DEFAULT/BULK]]
      MQ --> CW[Channel Workers]
      CW --> PREF[(Redis pref cache)]
      PREF -.-> PDB[(Postgres prefs+devices)]
      CW --> PROV[Providers APNs/FCM/SES/Twilio]
    end
    subgraph Events["Async / Analytics path"]
      PROV -.->|"callback"| CB[Callback Service]
      CB --> ES[[Analytics stream]]
      ES --> CH[(ClickHouse OLAP)]
    end
    CW --> MDB[(Postgres messages)]
    classDef client fill:#1a3a5c,stroke:#4A90D9,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    classDef svc fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    classDef queue fill:#3d2a0b,stroke:#B7791F,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    classDef db fill:#2a2f3a,stroke:#6B7280,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    classDef ext fill:#3a1a4a,stroke:#6B46C1,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    class P,U client
    class GW,ING,FO,CW,CB svc
    class LOG,MQ,ES queue
    class DDP,PREF,PDB,MDB,CH db
    class PROV ext
```

> 🎙️ Script: "Read end to end: producers and users hit the gateway. On the **accept path**, Ingestion dedups and durably appends triggers to Kafka, then acks — that's our durability guarantee. On the **fan-out/delivery path**, workers expand triggers into per-user Messages on priority queues, check preferences in Redis at the last moment, and hand off to per-channel providers. On the **async/analytics path**, provider callbacks update Message status and stream delivery events into ClickHouse for engagement metrics."

> 🤝 Checkpoint: "That's the skeleton end to end. Want me to go deep on the fan-out/spike handling, the delivery guarantees and dedup, or the preference/rate-limit hot path?"

## 7. Data Model & Storage

**PART A — ENTITY TABLE**

| Entity | Key fields (PK / shard key noted) | Chosen store | Shard/partition key | Consistency |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Notification | id (PK), eventType, templateId, priority, idempotencyKey (unique), createdAt | Postgres (+ Kafka log) | id | Strong on write |
| Message | id (PK), notificationId (FK), userId, channel, address, status, attempts | Postgres (sharded) | userId | Strong per-row |
| User/Device | userId (PK), deviceTokens[], emails[], phones[], platform, tokenValid | Postgres | userId | Strong |
| Preference | userId (PK), rules JSON, version | Postgres + Redis cache | userId | Read-your-writes via version |
| Template | id (PK), version, locale, subject, body | Postgres + CDN/edge cache | id | Eventual (cached) |
| DeliveryEvent | (messageId, ts, type) | ClickHouse | messageId/day | Eventual |
| Dedup key | idempotencyKey → notificationId | Redis (TTL 24h) | key | Strong (atomic) |

**PART B — STORAGE DECISION CARDS**

**🗄️ PostgreSQL — used for: Notification, Message, User/Device, Preference, Template**
1. What it is (plain words): a relational database that stores data in rows and columns with strict rules and guarantees a multi-step write either fully succeeds or fully rolls back.
2. Why we picked it — the access pattern that forces it: we need atomic, consistent updates to Message status and device/preference records, plus the idempotency unique constraint as a durable second dedup gate; relational integrity and transactions are exactly this.
3. What we considered instead and why we rejected each:
   - **DynamoDB** — great for KV by userId, but cross-entity transactions (notification + messages) and the unique idempotency constraint are awkward and add app-side complexity.
   - **MongoDB** — flexible schema is nice but we don't need it; we'd give up battle-tested relational tooling for no gain here.
   - **MySQL** — essentially equivalent; Postgres wins on JSONB (for preference rules) and modern ecosystem.
4. Trade-off we accept: we must shard Message/User by userId at this volume, so cross-shard joins are out — we denormalize what the workers need.
5. 🗣️ How to say it out loud: "Core records live in Postgres because I want a real unique constraint on the idempotency key and atomic status updates. I looked at Dynamo — perfect for the device lookup by user — but the cross-entity consistency and unique key are cleaner in Postgres. I shard by userId, so I just avoid cross-shard joins by denormalizing."

**🗄️ Kafka — used for: durable trigger log, priority queues, analytics stream**
1. What it is: an append-only, replayable log of events replicated across brokers so nothing is lost if a consumer is slow or crashes.
2. Why we picked it: the 50x spike requires a durable buffer with replay and ordered, partitioned consumption — this is the system's backbone.
3. Considered instead: **SQS/SNS** — simpler but weaker replay and ordering; **RabbitMQ** — fine at lower scale, harder to run at 600K/s with retention.
4. Trade-off: real operational cost to run and tune Kafka.
5. 🗣️ How to say it: "Kafka is the spine — it buffers the spike durably and lets me replay if a worker pool falls over. I'd reach for SQS at lower scale, but I want replay and partition ordering at six hundred K a second."

**🗄️ Redis — used for: dedup keys, preference cache, rate-limit counters**
1. What it is: an in-memory key-value store that's extremely fast and supports atomic operations.
2. Why we picked it: 600K preference reads/s and atomic frequency counters need sub-ms ops a database can't serve.
3. Considered instead: **Memcached** — no atomic counters/persistence; **in-process cache** — can't share rate state across workers.
4. Trade-off: it's a cache — staleness bounded by TTL, and we need persistence (AOF) for the rate counters.
5. 🗣️ How to say it: "Redis does three jobs — dedup, the preference cache, and the shared rate-limit counters — because all three need atomic, sub-millisecond ops across many workers."

**🗄️ ClickHouse — used for: DeliveryEvent analytics**
1. What it is: a columnar database built for fast aggregation over enormous tables.
2. Why we picked it: tens of thousands to millions of events/s and "how many delivered/opened" across billions of rows in sub-second.
3. Considered instead: **Postgres** — row store melts on these scans; **Elasticsearch** — costlier for pure numeric aggregates.
4. Trade-off: not for transactions or point lookups.
5. 🗣️ How to say it: "Engagement analytics go to ClickHouse because it's columnar — it aggregates billions of delivery events in well under a second, which Postgres can't touch."

**PART C — Per-operation consistency**

| Operation | Store | Consistency | Why |
|---|---|---|---|
| Write idempotency key | Redis + PG unique | Strong/atomic | Prevent double-send |
| Update Message status | Postgres | Strong per-row | Money/security correctness |
| Read preference on send | Redis (TTL) → PG | Read-your-writes via version | Honor just-changed opt-outs |
| Frequency counter incr | Redis atomic | Strong | Hard caps can't oversend |
| Read analytics | ClickHouse | Eventual (minutes) | Dashboards tolerate lag |

> 🎙️ Script: "The dominant store is Postgres for the core records — notifications, messages, devices, preferences — because I want a real unique constraint on idempotency and atomic status updates, sharded by userId. Kafka is the durable spine that absorbs the spike and feeds the workers. Redis pulls triple duty: dedup, the preference cache, and the shared rate-limit counters, all of which need atomic sub-millisecond ops. And the firehose of delivery events goes to ClickHouse, not Postgres — I considered keeping events in Postgres but at tens of thousands a second it would fall over, and ClickHouse aggregates them in sub-second."

## 8. Deep Dives — Bad → Good → Great

🆘 If you get stuck: go back to "accept durably, fan out off the hot path, deliver at-least-once with dedup, keep urgent on its own lane" — every dive traces to one of those.

### How do we handle a 50x broadcast spike without dropping or delaying anything?

**#### Bad: Synchronous fan-out in the request**
🗣️ What this is in plain words: when a broadcast comes in, expand it and send to everyone right there before replying.
Approach: Ingestion loops the audience and calls providers inline.
Why people try this: it's the simplest mental model.
⚠️ What breaks: a 10M-user broadcast at provider latencies (~100ms each) takes hours synchronously; the producer's request times out, and 120K/s of other triggers pile up behind it. The accept path dies.
🔁 What forces the upgrade: any broadcast bigger than a few thousand recipients.

**#### Good: Durable queue + autoscaling workers**
↩️ What the previous tier got wrong: it did heavy work on the request path; we move all of it behind a durable buffer.
🗣️ What this is in plain words: accept instantly, drop the work in a log, and let a fleet of workers chew through it as fast as they can scale.
Approach: Ingestion appends to Kafka and returns 202; Fan-out workers consume and expand in batches; channel workers autoscale on queue lag.
Why reasonable: decouples accept from delivery; absorbs spikes in the log.
⚠️ What breaks: a single huge broadcast still lands on one Kafka partition → one consumer becomes a hot shard while others idle; and bulk traffic shares lanes with urgent.
🔁 What forces the upgrade: hot-partition skew + need for priority isolation.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
  ING[Ingestion] -->|append| K[[Kafka]]
  K --> W1[Worker]
  K --> W2[Worker]
  K --> W3[Worker autoscaled]
  classDef svc fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,color:#e6edf3
  classDef queue fill:#3d2a0b,stroke:#B7791F,color:#e6edf3
  class ING,W1,W2,W3 svc
  class K queue
```

**#### Great: Sharded fan-out + priority lanes + batch expansion**
↩️ What the previous tier got wrong: one broadcast hammered one partition and bulk could starve urgent; we shard the expansion and physically separate priorities.
🗣️ What this is in plain words: break a giant broadcast into many chunks across partitions so many workers share it, and give urgent traffic its own road.
Approach: Fan-out reads a trigger, splits the audience into bounded chunks (e.g. 1K users each) and writes those chunks back as sub-jobs spread across partitions by a hash, so expansion parallelizes. Messages route to HIGH/DEFAULT/BULK topics with reserved HIGH workers. Workers batch provider calls (APNs/FCM accept batches) to cut per-message overhead.
🔢 Decision-forcing math: 10M-user broadcast ÷ 1K per chunk = 10K sub-jobs; across 200 workers that's 50 chunks each → minutes, not hours. HIGH lane: 30K/s ÷ ~2K msg/s per worker ≈ 15 workers reserved.
✅ Failure matrix:
| Scenario | What happens |
|---|---|
| Worker crash mid-chunk | Kafka redelivers chunk; idempotent by (notifId,userId,channel) → no dup |
| One partition hot | Sub-job hashing spreads load across partitions |
| Provider throttles | Per-channel token-bucket backpressure; retry with backoff |
| Spike beyond capacity | Log buffers; bulk lag grows but HIGH lane unaffected |
| Duplicate sub-job | Dedup on Message natural key suppresses |

> 🎙️ Script: "The spike answer is three moves. First, never expand on the request path — append to Kafka and ack. Second, shard the expansion: a ten-million broadcast becomes ten thousand thousand-user chunks hashed across partitions, so hundreds of workers share it and it finishes in minutes. Third, physically separate priority lanes with reserved workers so a marketing flood can't delay a 2FA code. Everything's idempotent on the message's natural key, so worker crashes redeliver without duplicating."
> 🧠 If they ask "why not just add workers?": "Adding workers doesn't help if all the work sits on one partition — the fix is spreading the keys, then scaling workers."

### How do we guarantee delivery without spamming (at-least-once + dedup)?

**#### Bad: Fire-and-forget, no retries**
🗣️ Plain words: send once, hope it lands.
⚠️ What breaks: provider blips drop ~1-3% of messages — including 2FA codes. Unacceptable for N3.
🔁 Forces upgrade: any durability requirement.

**#### Good: At-least-once with retries**
↩️ Previous tier lost messages on transient errors; we retry.
🗣️ Plain words: if a send fails, try again with growing delays.
Approach: on provider 5xx/timeout, exponential backoff retry (1s,2s,4s…) up to N attempts, then dead-letter queue.
⚠️ What breaks: retries can double-send — provider got it but its ack was lost → user gets two pushes (violates N5).
🔁 Forces upgrade: need duplicate suppression.

**#### Great: At-least-once + idempotent dedup → effectively-once**
↩️ Previous tier risked duplicates; we dedup at every layer.
🗣️ Plain words: we retry freely, but each message has a fingerprint so the same one never goes out twice.
Approach: (1) idempotency key dedups triggers at ingest (Redis + PG unique). (2) Each Message has a natural key (notifId,userId,channel); workers do compare-and-set on status QUEUED→SENDING so only one worker sends. (3) Use provider-side idempotency tokens where available (e.g. SES message dedup, Twilio idempotency). (4) Provider callbacks reconcile true status.
🔢 Decision-forcing math: with 3 retries and ~1% loss, undelivered drops to 1%³ ≈ 0.0001% — effectively guaranteed.
✅ Failure matrix:
| Scenario | What happens |
|---|---|
| Send ok, ack lost | Retry hits provider idempotency token → no dup; or CAS already SENT → skip |
| Worker dies after SENDING | Visibility timeout requeues; status CAS prevents second send |
| Permanent bad token | Mark FAILED, reap token, no retry |
| Duplicate trigger | Idempotency key short-circuits at ingest |
| Callback says bounced | Status BOUNCED, optional fallback channel |

> 🎙️ Script: "Delivery is at-least-once — I retry with backoff so transient provider failures don't lose anything. The risk is double-sending when a provider got the message but its ack was lost. I kill that with idempotency at three layers: the idempotency key dedups the trigger, a compare-and-set on the message status means only one worker can transition it to SENDING, and I pass provider-side dedup tokens where they exist. The net is effectively-once from the user's point of view — we retry aggressively but they never see a duplicate."
> 🧠 If they ask "so is it exactly-once?": "No system across third-party providers is truly exactly-once; it's at-least-once plus dedup, which gives effectively-once. The residual is a tiny window if a provider lacks idempotency tokens."

### How do we enforce per-user frequency caps at 600K/s?

**#### Good: Per-user counter in Redis**
🗣️ Plain words: keep a running tally per user per day; if they're over the cap, drop or digest.
Approach: atomic INCR on key `rate:{userId}:{type}:{day}` with EXPIRE; if > cap, suppress or route to a digest.
⚠️ What breaks: a single celebrity/system user (e.g. "all-users" type) becomes a hot key hammering one Redis shard.
🔁 Forces upgrade: hot-key skew.

**#### Great: Sharded counters + local pre-aggregation**
↩️ Previous tier had a hot key; we shard the counter and pre-aggregate in the worker.
🗣️ Plain words: split a hot user's counter into pieces and batch updates so no single key melts.
Approach: counter key suffixed with a shard `rate:{userId}:{type}:{day}:{shard}`, summed on read; workers batch local increments and flush periodically. Redis AOF persistence so a restart doesn't reset caps.
🔢 Decision-forcing math: 600K/s ÷ ~150K ops/s per node = ≥4 shards minimum; we run 8 for headroom.
> 🎙️ Script: "Frequency caps are atomic Redis counters keyed by user, type, and day, with a TTL. The trap is a hot key — a system-wide type all hitting one user-ish key — so I shard the counter and let workers batch increments locally before flushing. I persist Redis with AOF so a restart can't wipe the caps and accidentally let a blast through."
> 🧠 If they ask "what if Redis is down?": "Caps fail open for transactional/security traffic and fail closed for marketing — I'd rather under-send marketing than spam, but never block a 2FA code."

### How do we manage dead/expired device tokens (CDN-style global delivery)?

**#### Great: Provider feedback + token reaping + regional routing**
🗣️ Plain words: when a provider says a token is dead, stop using it; route users to the nearest provider edge.
Approach: APNs/FCM return "unregistered/invalid"; the callback marks the token invalid so future fan-out skips it. For global low latency we deploy ingestion + workers multi-region and let providers' own global edges (APNs/FCM are globally distributed) handle last-mile; email/SMS pick regional providers.
✅ Failure matrix:
| Scenario | What happens |
|---|---|
| Dead token | Marked invalid, skipped, user prompted to re-register on next app open |
| Region down | Geo-route producers to healthy region; Kafka MirrorMaker replicates |
| Provider regional outage | Failover to secondary provider for that channel |

> 🎙️ Script: "Tokens go stale constantly, so I treat provider 'unregistered' responses as the source of truth — mark the token dead and stop sending. For global reach, push providers are already globally distributed, so I lean on their edges and just run my ingestion and workers multi-region for resilience, with a secondary provider per channel to fail over to."

## 9. Reliability, Failure Modes & Cost

**9A — Availability targets (what the "nines" mean):**
Accept/ingest path targets **four nines (99.99%)** = ~52 minutes downtime/year — losing a trigger is the worst outcome, so this path is the most protected. Delivery path targets **99.95%** = ~4.4 hours/year (delivery can lag without "failing"). Mechanisms: stateless ingestion behind a load balancer across **multi-AZ** (multiple data centres in one region, so if one building loses power another takes over automatically); Kafka replicated 3x across AZs; workers autoscaled and stateless.

**9B — Per-dependency failure table:**

| Component | What breaks if down | Graceful degradation (what user sees) | Recovery |
|---|---|---|---|
| API Gateway | No new triggers accepted | Producers get 503 + retry; no data lost | Multi-AZ replicas; auto-failover |
| Kafka | Can't accept or deliver | Ingestion returns 503 (won't ack without durability) — producers retry | 3x replication; broker auto-recover |
| Redis (pref/dedup) | Slower checks | Fall back to Postgres reads; caps fail open for transactional | Replicas + AOF restore |
| Postgres | Status/pref writes blocked | Workers buffer status in Kafka, replay later; sends continue | Multi-AZ primary failover |
| A provider (e.g. Twilio) | That channel stalls | Messages retry/backoff; fail over to secondary provider; user still gets push/email | Circuit breaker + secondary |
| ClickHouse | Analytics stale | Dashboards lag; delivery unaffected (events buffer in Kafka) | Replay from stream |

Circuit breakers wrap every provider (open after error threshold → stop hammering a dead provider, send to secondary); retries use exponential backoff with jitter to avoid thundering herds.

**9C — RPO / RTO per data class:**
RPO (Recovery Point Objective) = how much data we're OK losing in a crash right now. RTO (Recovery Time Objective) = how long until service is back.

| Data class | RPO | RTO | Mechanism |
|---|---|---|---|
| Accepted triggers | ≈0 | < 1 min | Kafka 3x sync replication before ack |
| Message status | seconds | < 5 min | Postgres multi-AZ + status replay from Kafka |
| Preferences/devices | ≈0 | < 5 min | Postgres synchronous replica |
| Frequency counters | seconds | < 1 min | Redis AOF persistence |
| DeliveryEvents | minutes | minutes | Rebuildable by replaying Kafka |

**9D — Cost breakdown:**
Top drivers: (1) **Provider fees** — SMS especially (Twilio charges per message; at hundreds of millions of SMS this dwarfs infrastructure) — the dominant line item. (2) **Kafka + worker compute** during spikes (autoscaling fleet). (3) **ClickHouse storage/compute** for the events firehose. Rough order: provider fees in the high six/seven figures/month at this scale, infra a fraction of that. Biggest optimization: **channel downgrade & batching** — prefer free push over paid SMS where the user allows, and batch/digest low-priority messages to cut volume.

> 🎙️ Script: "When a provider like Twilio goes down, the user still gets the push and email — that channel just retries and fails over to a secondary, they don't notice. If Kafka or the accept path has trouble, producers get a 503 and retry, so we never silently lose a trigger — that's the whole point of acking only after durable replication. And if analytics breaks, delivery doesn't even blink; events just buffer and replay. The scary cost is SMS, so the biggest lever is routing to push when we can."

## 10. Trade-off Ledger

**🔀 Decision: Async 202-accept vs synchronous delivery**
1. What we chose and why: accept + durable enqueue + 202, deliver async — survives 50x spikes.
2. What we gave up: callers can't know delivery result synchronously; they poll/subscribe.
3. 🗣️ Plain words: like dropping a letter in a guaranteed mailbox instead of waiting at the post office for it to arrive.
4. When this reverses: if volume were tiny and callers needed synchronous confirmation, a direct synchronous send would be simpler.
5. 🗣️ How to say it: "I ack on durable enqueue, not on delivery — it's the only way to absorb spikes and guarantee nothing's lost."

**🔀 Decision: At-least-once + dedup vs exactly-once**
1. Chose at-least-once with idempotent dedup.
2. Gave up: a tiny residual duplicate window where providers lack idempotency tokens.
3. 🗣️ Plain words: a fast highway with a guardrail beats a guaranteed-but-impossible perfect road.
4. When this reverses: if we owned the delivery channel end-to-end we could do true exactly-once.
5. 🗣️ How to say it: "Exactly-once across third parties is a myth, so I do at-least-once plus dedup for effectively-once."

**🔀 Decision: Postgres (sharded) vs NoSQL for core records**
1. Chose Postgres for unique idempotency constraint + atomic status.
2. Gave up: cross-shard joins; manual sharding ops.
3. 🗣️ Plain words: chose strict bookkeeping over schema flexibility we don't need.
4. When this reverses: if access were pure KV-by-user with no transactions, DynamoDB would be simpler and more elastic.
5. 🗣️ How to say it: "Postgres for the real unique constraint and atomic updates; I shard by userId and avoid cross-shard joins."

**🔀 Decision: Separate priority lanes vs single weighted queue**
1. Chose physically separate topics + reserved HIGH workers.
2. Gave up: some idle reserved capacity for HIGH.
3. 🗣️ Plain words: a dedicated ambulance lane instead of hoping traffic parts.
4. When this reverses: if there were no urgent class, one queue is cheaper.
5. 🗣️ How to say it: "Urgent gets its own road and reserved workers so a marketing blast can never delay a login code."

> 🎙️ Script: "The decisions I'd defend hardest: ack on durable enqueue not delivery — that's the spike and durability story; at-least-once plus dedup because exactly-once across third parties isn't real; Postgres for the idempotency constraint and atomic status even though I pay for sharding; and physically separate priority lanes because weighting a single queue still lets bulk starve urgent under enough load. Each flips only if scale or scope drops — tiny volume or no urgent class would let me simplify."

## 11. Likely Interviewer Questions & Answers

**❓ "Two retries fire for the same message — does the user get it twice?"**
The mechanism: each Message has a natural key (notifId,userId,channel) and a status the worker advances via compare-and-set QUEUED→SENDING; only one worker wins, others skip. Provider-side idempotency tokens catch the ack-lost case.
🗣️ In plain words: every message has a fingerprint, so even if we try twice only one actually goes out.
💬 One-liner: "At-least-once for safety, dedup on a natural key for effectively-once."

**❓ "Redis goes down during a flash sale — what happens?"**
The mechanism: preference checks fall back to Postgres (slower but correct); rate counters fail open for transactional/security and fail closed for marketing so we under-send rather than spam; dedup leans on the Postgres unique constraint.
🗣️ In plain words: we get slower and a bit more conservative, but we never spam and never block a login code.
💬 One-liner: "Degrade to Postgres, fail marketing closed, keep 2FA flowing."

**❓ "A promo blasts 10M users — how do you not delay 2FA?"**
The mechanism: 2FA rides a separate HIGH Kafka topic with reserved workers; the broadcast is chunked across partitions on BULK lanes. The lanes never share workers.
🗣️ In plain words: urgent traffic drives on an empty dedicated road.
💬 One-liner: "Separate topics, reserved workers — physical isolation, not weighting."

**❓ "Can you oversell a frequency cap — send 3 when the cap is 1?"**
The mechanism: atomic Redis INCR on a per-user-per-day key gates the send; sharded counters summed on read prevent hot-key races; AOF persistence survives restarts.
🗣️ In plain words: a running tally that's checked atomically, so we can't go over.
💬 One-liner: "Atomic counters with TTL — the cap is enforced at send time, not best-effort."

**❓ "How do you stop a producer emailing the wrong person or forging content?"**
The mechanism: producers send only eventType + userId + templateData + idempotencyKey; the server resolves the address from its own store and renders from a server-side Template. Caller identity from the service JWT, never the body.
🗣️ In plain words: the producer says who and why, never the email address or the words — we fill those in.
💬 One-liner: "Content and address are server-resolved; the client can't forge either."

**❓ "What's the most expensive part and how would you cut it?"**
The mechanism: SMS provider fees dominate at scale. We cut cost by channel-downgrading to push/email when preferences allow, batching/digesting low-priority messages, and reaping dead tokens so we don't pay to send into the void.
🗣️ In plain words: texting costs real money per message, so we prefer free push and bundle low-urgency stuff.
💬 One-liner: "SMS is the cost driver — route to push and digest the rest."

**❓ "A provider (Twilio) has a regional outage — what do users experience?"**
The mechanism: a circuit breaker opens after an error threshold and we fail over to a secondary SMS provider; in-flight messages retry with backoff; other channels are unaffected.
🗣️ In plain words: texts reroute through a backup carrier; the user still gets their push and email.
💬 One-liner: "Circuit-break, fail over to a secondary provider, retry the rest."

**❓ "How would you add a new channel, like WhatsApp, later?"**
The mechanism: channels are isolated behind provider adapters and a channel-worker pool; adding one means a new adapter + queue + preference option — no change to ingestion or fan-out.
🗣️ In plain words: each channel is a plug-in, so a new one slots in without touching the core.
💬 One-liner: "New channel = new adapter + queue; the core is channel-agnostic."

**❓ "Can the same order trigger two notifications if the order service retries?"**
The mechanism: the idempotencyKey is deduped at ingest (Redis set-if-absent + Postgres unique constraint); a retry returns the original 202 without re-enqueueing.
🗣️ In plain words: the trigger has a unique ticket number; the second copy is recognized and ignored.
💬 One-liner: "Idempotency key at ingest — retries collapse to one notification."

**❓ "How fresh are analytics — can a PM trust the open-rate now?"**
The mechanism: delivery events stream to ClickHouse via a consumer with minutes of lag; if the consumer lags, events buffer in Kafka, so numbers are complete but slightly delayed.
🗣️ In plain words: the dashboard is a few minutes behind but never wrong or missing data.
💬 One-liner: "Near-real-time, eventually complete — analytics never block delivery."

> 🎙️ 60-second verbal summary: "A notification system is a fan-out and delivery engine: producers fire events and we deliver them across push, email, and SMS while honoring each user's preferences. The design splits into two halves. The accept half is tiny and bulletproof — a stateless ingestion service dedups on an idempotency key and durably appends every trigger to Kafka before acking with a 202, so we never lose anything even under a fifty-times spike. The delivery half is where the hard problems live — fan-out workers expand a trigger into per-user, per-channel messages on priority-separated lanes, check preferences in Redis at the last moment, and hand off to per-channel providers with at-least-once retries plus idempotent dedup for effectively-once delivery, keeping 2FA on a reserved lane so a marketing blast can never delay a login code. The two halves connect through Kafka, which also streams every delivery event into ClickHouse for engagement analytics off the hot path. Every hard decision traces back to one principle: accept durably and instantly, then deliver asynchronously across flaky third parties without ever spamming the user."
